turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Now, I will summarize the dissent that Justice Ginsburg, Sotomayor and I have made. There are many thousands of individuals who are involved in this case. They are all persons who believe they have a right to enter into or remain in the United States. A sizeable number of them turn out to be right and they stay here and they have a right to stay here. The government recognizes that there is a reasonable possibility that they are right, from the beginning the government recognizes that, but it holds them confined in jails or in prisons for months, sometimes for years until the matter can be resolved. They spend those months imprisoned, without bail, for the government reads the statute as denying them the bail hearings that the law makes available even for those accused of the most serious crimes. This Court, I think, for the first time ever, reads a statute, the statute before us, similarly as permitting long-term confinement of a person here in the United States without an opportunity to obtain bail. By the way, an opportunity doesn't mean that they are necessarily going to be released or there may be a risk of flight or harm that would justify denying them bail. I think the majority is wrong to read the statute as not even giving them a hearing, a chance to show that they are entitled to bail. But if it were right, which I think it isn't, then rather than send this case back to the lower courts for months and months of further proceedings, it should decide the constitutional question now for the constitutional question has been briefed in this Court and in my view we should then hold the statute unconstitutional. Why is it likely unconstitutional? Well, because the Constitution forbids depriving any person of his or her liberty without due process of law, because to deny a person, a person not even accused of a crime, an opportunity to obtain bail and forcing that person to remain in prison in the United States, away from friends, family, community for months, perhaps years, is arbitrary and a deprivation of liberty without due process of law. Because at the time of the founders, Blackstone tells us, the Common Law of England permitted any prisoner before conviction to seek bail, because the colonists brought that right to the United States and from that time to this every criminal defendant has had the right to seek bail, because that right should apply a fortiori here where the individuals before us are not criminal defendants and seen as a group, and compared with criminal defendants they are no more likely to flee than are those accused of serious crimes and they are less likely to risk harm to the community. Because this Court has previously said that bail is basic to our system of law and because this Court's prior cases make clear that we have consistently read similar statutes as authorizing bail. These constitutional considerations require us, at the least, to follow the well-established legal principle that adds "between two possible interpretations of the statute, by one of which it would be unconstitutional and by the other valid, our plain duty is to adopt that which will save the act." And a constitutional interpretation of the statute is in our view certainly possible. In respect to many of the individuals involved, the key statutory words appear in a sentence that says the alien shall be detained for further consideration. It says nothing about bail there. The word is detained and that can be weaned under restraint and therefore consistent with bail, which typically involves not just release from jail, but release under some, often many, restraining conditions. Indeed, our precedent treats the word detention and a similar word custody as consistent with bail. The government's own Board of Immigration Appeals has taken a similar position and there is nothing in the statute or in its history that says that Congress thought about and intended to deny bail proceedings. In respect to some other individuals here, there is a statutory provision that says the Attorney General may release that alien only if he decides that doing so is necessary to protect the witness. This provision however has to do with a special government program called the Witness Protection Program and for reasons spelled out in the dissent, it has nothing to do with the individuals or circumstances at issue here. Now, the dissent is long, for it does try to dot every legal i and cross every t, but the reader can best understand the dissent by recalling a few basic principles. You have to recall the words of the Declaration of Independence, its insistence that all men and women have certain inalienable rights. We have to recall that among those rights is what it says is liberty. We just have to remember that the Constitution's Due Process Clause protect each person's liberty from arbitrary deprivation and we need only keep in mind the fact that since Blackstone's time, indeed since long before, liberty has included the right of a confined, unconvicted person to seek release on bail. It is not difficult, in our view, to read the words of the statute as consistent with that basic right. I would find it far more difficult, indeed perhaps alarming, to believe that Congress wrote these statutory words in order to put thousands of individuals at risk of lengthy confinement all within the United States, but all without hope of bail. I think we should read the statute as requiring the protection of the basic right to seek bail and for these reasons set forth I am afraid at greater length in our opinion, we respectfully dissent.
